# Git and GitHub for Solo Developers {#sec-git-solo}

::: {.callout-note}
## Sources

Adapted from author's lecture notes and supporting materials for a graduate practicum in biostatistics.
:::

## Prerequisites

Answer the following questions to see if you can bypass this
chapter. You can find the answers at the end of the chapter in
@sec-quiz-answers-05.

1. Why should a solo biostatistician use Git for a project
   that no one else will ever see?
2. What is the difference between a commit, a branch, and a
   tag?
3. Name one common scenario in which `git stash` saves you
   from data loss.

## Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you should be able to:

- Initialise a Git repository, make commits, and push to
  GitHub from the command line.
- Use branches to experiment without risking your main line
  of work.
- Resolve a merge conflict in R source.
- Authenticate to GitHub with an SSH key or a personal access
  token.
- Use `git tag` to mark release points (e.g., submission to a
  journal).

## Orientation

Git is the time machine you wish you had the last time you
overwrote a working analysis. For a solo developer it is
still worth the learning investment: the branching workflow
lets you try risky refactors without fear, and the history
lets you answer 'when did this break?' in seconds rather than
hours.

## Why Git for solo work

TODO: time-travel, branching for experiments, irreversible
pre-submission tagging.

## Minimum viable Git

TODO: init, add, commit, log, status, diff.

## Branching and merging

TODO: `branch`, `switch`, `merge`, `rebase`. When to prefer
each.

## Remotes: GitHub, GitLab, Gitea

TODO: push, pull, fetch, clone.

## Authentication: SSH and PAT

TODO: setting up an SSH key; creating a PAT.

## Tagging for submissions

TODO: `git tag -a v1.0 -m 'submitted to JBS'`.

## When things go wrong

TODO: `stash`, `reset`, `reflog`, `checkout` of old files.

## With AI assistance {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Prompts to try

1. Paste `git log --oneline -10` and `git status` and ask the
   LLM to summarise the state of your repo in two sentences.
2. Ask the LLM to resolve a synthetic merge conflict pasted
   inline. Verify the proposed resolution against what you
   would pick.
3. Ask the LLM to describe what `git reflog` recovers in three
   different scenarios. Check the answer against the official
   `git-reflog` documentation.
:::

## Exercises

1. Create a new GitHub repository, clone it locally, add a
   single `.R` file, and push one commit. Verify the commit
   appears on GitHub.
2. Create and resolve a merge conflict intentionally: make a
   branch, edit line 1 of `README.md`, commit, switch back to
   main, edit line 1 differently, commit, and merge.
3. Tag a completed analysis with `v1.0` and push the tag to
   GitHub. Verify the tagged release is visible in the GitHub
   UI.

## Further reading

- [@bryan2019happygit] --- the standard reference for Git in R workflows.
- Pro Git by Chacon and Straub at `git-scm.com/book` --- the canonical free Git book.

## Prerequisites answers {#sec-quiz-answers-05}

1. Even for solo work, Git provides: a time machine (roll
   back to any prior state), a branching workflow (try risky
   refactors without fear), a structured log of what you did
   and when, and an off-site backup if you push to a remote.
   The cost is a one-time learning curve; the benefit
   compounds for the rest of your career.
2. A commit is a snapshot of the repository at a point in
   time, with a parent commit, an author, a message, and a
   unique hash. A branch is a movable pointer to a commit,
   used to develop features or experiments in isolation. A
   tag is an immovable pointer to a commit, used to mark
   release points.
3. You are in the middle of editing files but need to pull
   changes from the remote or switch to another branch
   urgently. `git stash` puts your uncommitted work
   temporarily aside; `git stash pop` restores it later. This
   avoids committing half-finished work just to unblock a
   branch switch.
